introduction.p: Chapter answered the scale-factor question left by Chapter . Cylindrical coordinates needed the factor r, and spherical coordinates needed the factor \rho^2\sin\phi. Those were not special tricks. They were Jacobian determinants.

subsection.title: Skills: choosing and using substitutions
subsection.p: A good substitution usually comes from the boundary equations.
If the boundary contains x+y=1 \qquad\text{and}\qquad x+y=4, then the expression u=x+y is asking to become a coordinate.
If the boundary contains xy=1 \qquad\text{and}\qquad xy=5, then u=xy may be a natural coordinate.
If the region is circular, polar coordinates are listening. If the solid is spherical, spherical coordinates are listening. If the object is an ellipsoid, a stretched ball is often hiding inside it.

table.title: Natural substitutions suggested by the geometry

subsection.p: The absolute value is for size. The sign is for orientation.

figure.caption: A substitution is both a geometric choice and a measurement correction.

subsection.title: Worked review example: a slanted window
subsection.p: A thin glass window occupies the parallelogram-like region R=\{(x,y):1\le x+y\le3,\ 0\le x-y\le2\}. The density of tint on the glass is q(x,y)=x+y. Find the total amount of tint: \iint_R (x+y)\,dA.
The boundary equations suggest u=x+y, \qquad v=x-y. Then R becomes the rectangle 1\le u\le3, \qquad 0\le v\le2.

Solve for x and y: x=\frac{u+v}{2}, \qquad y=\frac{u-v}{2}. The Jacobian determinant is
\frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)} \amp= \det\begin{pmatrix} 1/2\amp 1/2\\ 1/2\amp -1/2\end{pmatrix} \amp= -\frac14-\frac14 \amp= -\frac12.
So the ordinary area factor is \left|\frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}\right|=\frac12. The integrand becomes x+y=u. Therefore
\iint_R (x+y)\,dA \amp= \int_1^3\int_0^2 u\cdot\frac12\,dv\,du \amp= \int_1^3 u\,du \amp= \left[\frac{u^2}{2}\right]_1^3 \amp= 4.
Thus \boxed{\iint_R (x+y)\,dA=4.}

The signed Jacobian was negative. That means the ordered variables (u,v) reverse the usual orientation of the xy-plane. The glass does not have negative area, so the ordinary integral uses the absolute value.

subsection.title: Worked review example: a curved plate between hyperbolas
subsection.p: A thin metal plate lies in the first quadrant. Its edges are xy=1,\qquad xy=4, and \frac{y}{x}=1,\qquad \frac{y}{x}=3. Its density is \delta(x,y)=xy. Find its mass.
The boundary equations almost name the new variables: u=xy, \qquad v=\frac{y}{x}. Then the plate becomes the rectangle 1\le u\le4, \qquad 1\le v\le3.

Because we are in the first quadrant, x\gt0 and y\gt0. From u=xy, \qquad v=\frac{y}{x}, we get y=vx. Then u=x(vx)=vx^2, so x=\sqrt{\frac{u}{v}}. Also, y=vx=\sqrt{uv}. Thus x=\sqrt{\frac{u}{v}}, \qquad y=\sqrt{uv}.

Compute the Jacobian. A quick way is to compute the determinant of the forward map (x,y)\longmapsto (u,v). Since u=xy, \qquad v=\frac{y}{x}, we have \frac{\partial(u,v)}{\partial(x,y)}=\det\begin{pmatrix} y\amp x\\ -y/x^2\amp 1/x\end{pmatrix}. Therefore
\frac{\partial(u,v)}{\partial(x,y)} \amp= y\cdot\frac1x - x\left(-\frac{y}{x^2}\right) \amp= \frac{y}{x}+\frac{y}{x} \amp= 2\frac{y}{x} \amp= 2v.
So \boxed{\frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}=\frac{1}{2v}.} The density becomes \delta(x,y)=xy=u. Thus
M \amp= \iint_R xy\,dA \amp= \int_1^4\int_1^3 u\cdot\frac{1}{2v}\,dv\,du \amp= \frac12 \left(\int_1^4 u\,du\right)\left(\int_1^3 \frac{1}{v}\,dv\right) \amp= \frac12 \left[\frac{u^2}{2}\right]_1^4 \left[\ln v\right]_1^3 \amp= \frac12\cdot\frac{15}{2}\cdot\ln 3 \amp= \frac{15}{4}\ln 3.
So \boxed{M=\frac{15}{4}\ln 3.}
The region looked awkward in x,y. It was a rectangle in the variables its own edges were already using.

subsection.title: Worked review example: a stretched ball
subsection.p: Let E be the ellipsoid \frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{9}+z^2\le1. Its density is \delta(x,y,z)=1+\frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{9}+z^2. Find the mass.
The ellipsoid is a stretched unit ball. Use x=2u, \qquad y=3v, \qquad z=w. Then \frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{9}+z^2=u^2+v^2+w^2. So the solid E becomes the unit ball B=\{(u,v,w):u^2+v^2+w^2\le1\}.

The Jacobian determinant is \det\begin{pmatrix} 2\amp0\amp0\\ 0\amp3\amp0\\ 0\amp0\amp1\end{pmatrix}=6. The density becomes \delta=1+u^2+v^2+w^2. Therefore
M \amp= \iiint_E \left(1+\frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{9}+z^2\right)dV \amp= \iiint_B 6(1+u^2+v^2+w^2)\,du\,dv\,dw.
Two coordinate changes worked together. The first turned the ellipsoid into a ball. The second used spherical coordinates because the new ball had radial symmetry.

statement.p: What does the Jacobian determinant measure geometrically in a plane change of variables?
What does the Jacobian determinant measure geometrically in a space change of variables?
Why does an ordinary area or volume integral use |J| instead of J?
What information is kept by dx\wedge dy but forgotten by dA?
What information is kept by dx\wedge dy\wedge dz but forgotten by dV?
In the plane, why does T^*(dx\wedge dy)=\frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}\,du\wedge dv contain the signed Jacobian, not its absolute value?
Why must a change of variables be one-to-one on the interior of the parameter region?
Why are boundary overlaps usually harmless?
Why does polar coordinates having J=r=0 at r=0 not ruin the area formula for a disk?
What goes wrong if a transformation has J=0 on a whole two-dimensional or three-dimensional patch?
Why should a map with a crease be split into smooth pieces before applying the change-of-variables theorem?
What does it mean to say that polar coordinates with 0\le\theta\le4\pi cover a disk with multiplicity 2?
How can the equations of a region's boundary suggest useful new variables?
What is the difference between changing the integrand and changing the area or volume element?
Why is the determinant rule a local rule, while the one-to-one condition is a global condition?
For x=3u-v, \qquad y=u+2v, compute \frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}.
Let S be the unit square 0\le u\le1,\qquad 0\le v\le1. Under x=3u-v, \qquad y=u+2v, find the area of T(S).
Let R be the image of the unit square under x=2u+v, \qquad y=u+4v. Compute \iint_R (x-y)\,dA.
Let u=x+y, \qquad v=x-y. Find x and y in terms of u and v, and compute \frac{\partial(x,y)}{\partial(u,v)}.
Let R=\{(x,y):0\le x+y\le2,\ 1\le x-y\le3\}. Use u=x+y, \qquad v=x-y to find the area of R.
Use polar coordinates to compute the area of the disk x^2+y^2\le a^2.
Use polar coordinates to compute \iint_R (x^2+y^2)\,dA, where R is the annulus 1\le x^2+y^2\le4.
A thin circular plate has radius 3 and density \delta(x,y)=5-x^2-y^2. Use polar coordinates to find its mass.
Let R be the first-quadrant region bounded by xy=2,\qquad xy=6, and \frac{y}{x}=1,\qquad \frac{y}{x}=4. Use u=xy, \qquad v=\frac{y}{x} to find the area of R.
For the same region as the previous problem, compute \iint_R xy\,dA.
Let T(u,v)=(u^2-v^2,\ 2uv). Compute T^*(dx\wedge dy). Where does the Jacobian vanish?
Explain why the map T(u,v)=(u^2-v^2,\ 2uv) covers most of its image twice if the domain is a disk centered at the origin.
Let T(u,v)=(u,\ 1-v). Compute T^*(dx\wedge dy). Does this map preserve or reverse orientation?
Let T(u,v,w)=(2u,\ 3v,\ 4w). Find the volume scale factor.
Use the substitution x=2u,\qquad y=3v,\qquad z=4w to find the volume of the ellipsoid \frac{x^2}{4}+\frac{y^2}{9}+\frac{z^2}{16}\le1.
Use cylindrical coordinates to compute the volume of the solid cylinder x^2+y^2\le9, \qquad 0\le z\le5.
Use spherical coordinates to compute the volume of the ball x^2+y^2+z^2\le a^2.
A ball of radius 2 has density \delta(\rho)=4-\rho. Use spherical coordinates to find its mass.
Let T(u,v,w)=(u,\ v,\ uvw). Compute the Jacobian determinant.
Use x=u,\qquad y=v,\qquad z=u(1+v)w to find the volume of E=\{(x,y,z):1\le x\le2,\ 0\le y\le1,\ 0\le z\le x(1+y)\}.
Let x=r\cos\theta, \qquad y=r\sin\theta. Compute dx\wedge dy in terms of dr and d\theta.
Let x=r\cos\theta, \qquad y=r\sin\theta, \qquad z=z. Compute dx\wedge dy\wedge dz in terms of dr,d\theta,dz.
Let x=\rho\sin\phi\cos\theta, \quad y=\rho\sin\phi\sin\theta, \quad z=\rho\cos\phi. State the volume element in spherical coordinates.
Decide whether the following parameter region covers its image once, twice, or with boundary overlap only: 0\le r\le2,\qquad 0\le\theta\le2\pi. Explain.
Decide whether the following parameter region covers its image once, twice, or with boundary overlap only: 0\le r\le2,\qquad 0\le\theta\le4\pi. Explain.
Choose useful variables for the region bounded by y=2x,\qquad y=5x,\qquad x^2+y^2=1,\qquad x^2+y^2=9. Do not evaluate an integral. Just explain the substitution.
Choose useful variables for the region bounded by x+y=1,\qquad x+y=4,\qquad x-2y=0,\qquad x-2y=3. Do not evaluate an integral. Just explain the substitution.
Write the ordinary and oriented change-of-variables rules for a plane transformation.
Write the ordinary and oriented change-of-variables rules for a space transformation.
Give an example of a transformation whose Jacobian is correct locally but whose parameter region covers the same image twice.

subsection.title: Project: Gaussian integrals and polar coordinates
subsection.p: The integral \int e^{-x^2}\,dx has no elementary antiderivative. That is a single-variable problem. The surprising way through it is to square the integral and make it two-dimensional.
Let I=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2}\,dx. This integral is positive, so if we can find I^2, then we can find I.

subsubsection.title: Part 1. Square the integral
subsubsection.p: Write I^2=\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2}\,dx\right)\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-y^2}\,dy\right). The second variable is called y so the two copies can be combined as a double integral: \boxed{I^2=\iint_{\mathbb R^2} e^{-(x^2+y^2)}\,dA.} The integrand is now radial: e^{-(x^2+y^2)}=e^{-r^2}. The region is the whole plane. Polar coordinates fit both the integrand and the region.

subsubsection.title: Part 2. Change to polar coordinates
subsubsection.p:
In polar coordinates, dA=r\,dr\,d\theta. Therefore I^2=\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^\infty e^{-r^2}r\,dr\,d\theta. The inner integral uses the substitution s=r^2, \qquad ds=2r\,dr. So
\int_0^\infty e^{-r^2}r\,dr \amp= \frac12\int_0^\infty e^{-s}\,ds \amp= \frac12.
Thus
I^2 \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\frac12\,d\theta \amp= \pi.
Since I\gt0, \boxed{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2}\,dx=\sqrt{\pi}.}
This is one of the most famous uses of polar coordinates. A one-variable antiderivative was unavailable, but a two-dimensional symmetry solved the definite integral.

subsubsection.title: Part 3. A careful finite-radius version
subsubsection.p:
For a radius R\gt0, define G(R)=\iint_{x^2+y^2\le R^2} e^{-(x^2+y^2)}\,dA. Use polar coordinates:
G(R) \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^R e^{-r^2}r\,dr\,d\theta \amp= 2\pi\left[-\frac12e^{-r^2}\right]_0^R \amp= \pi(1-e^{-R^2}).
So \boxed{G(R)=\pi(1-e^{-R^2}).} As R\to\infty, G(R)\to\pi. That is the polar-coordinate shadow of I^2=\pi.

subsubsection.title: Part 4. A scaled Gaussian
Part 5. Normalizing the normal density
subsubsection.p: A probability density on the real line must integrate to 1. The bell-shaped density with mean 0 and spread parameter \sigma\gt0 has the form p(x)=C e^{-x^2/(2\sigma^2)}. Find C.

Using the scaled Gaussian formula with a=\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}, we get \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}e^{-x^2/(2\sigma^2)}\,dx=\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}=\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}. Therefore 1=C\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}, so \boxed{C=\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}.} Thus the normalized density is \boxed{p(x)=\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/(2\sigma^2)}.}

subsubsection.title: What to hand in
subsubsection.p: Write a short report with the following pieces.

li.p: Explain why the single-variable antiderivative is not the point of this problem.
Show how squaring the integral produces a double integral over \mathbb R^2.
Evaluate the double integral using polar coordinates.
Use the result to find the normalizing constant for C e^{-x^2/(2\sigma^2)}.
Write one paragraph explaining why polar coordinates were the right coordinates for the squared integral.

conclusion.title: Hook: the boundary of a region now begins to matter
conclusion.p: Change of variables taught us to respect the inside of a region. Tiny squares became parallelograms. Tiny boxes became parallelepipeds. The determinant kept track of how much size changed, and the wedge product kept track of orientation.

But the warning examples kept pointing toward the boundary. A square reflected by (u,v)\mapsto (u,1-v) did not change its ordinary area, but it reversed the direction in which its boundary was walked. A polar rectangle with 0\le\theta\le4\pi did not describe a larger disk; it walked around the same disk twice.

So far, the boundary has mostly helped us write bounds. Soon it will become an object we integrate over. A force field can do work along a path. A swirling fluid can have circulation around a closed curve. A 1-form P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz is built to be added along such a path.
